skill.name: modal
skill.description: Build, deploy, and operate Modal serverless Python workloads with containers, GPUs, autoscaling, volumes, secrets, web endpoints, and scheduled jobs.
skill.summary: Deploy Python workloads on Modal with GPU compute, autoscaling, storage, secrets, APIs, and schedules.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a 100/100 risk score because the skill is documentation for Modal operations: CLI commands, remote function execution, image build commands, secrets, network endpoints, and file storage. Review found no prompt injection attempt, malicious exfiltration logic, or executable skill code; many high-severity hits are false positives such as torch model.eval(), GPU names, markdown file extensions, and fixed command examples. The skill is publishable with a medium-risk warning because users may copy examples that run code in cloud containers, expose endpoints, or mishandle credentials.

medium_findings.title: Remote Code Execution and Cloud Deployment Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally teaches Modal commands and decorators that execute user Python in cloud containers. The reviewed examples use expected Modal patterns and fixed subprocess arguments, but copied examples can deploy persistent services or run costly jobs if used without approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines clearly show Modal authentication, subprocess execution with fixed arguments, and a web server process. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it is operationally sensitive cloud execution guidance.

medium_findings.title: Secret and Environment Variable Handling Requires Care
medium_findings.description: The documentation explains how to inject secrets and read credentials from environment variables. This is expected Modal usage, but examples include placeholder API key handling and should be treated as sensitive guidance, not copied with real secrets hardcoded.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code samples directly access environment variables and pass API keys into functions. There is no evidence of exfiltration, but accidental credential exposure is a realistic misuse risk.

medium_findings.title: Local File Inclusion and Persistent Writes Can Expose Data
medium_findings.description: The skill shows adding local directories to images and writing files to Modal volumes. These are legitimate features, but the local-directory example could include sensitive local files if copied with credential paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples show real file inclusion and persistent writes. The risk depends on user-provided paths and data, so this is a contextual misuse risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: External Network and Public Endpoint Patterns
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples for public Modal endpoint URLs, outbound API requests, and private registry access. These capabilities are expected, but they can disclose data or create public attack surfaces if authentication and rate controls are not applied.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is documented openly and aligns with Modal use cases. I did not find hidden destinations or malicious domains, but endpoint and outbound request examples are security-sensitive.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Eval Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts are caused by documentation text such as markdown references, GPU model names, or unrelated words. The eval alert points to torch model.eval(), which switches a model to inference mode and is not dynamic code evaluation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain ordinary documentation text, GPU labels, or torch model.eval(). No evidence of weak cryptographic algorithm usage or dynamic eval() code execution was found.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL and CLI Command Alerts Are Expected Documentation
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs and shell commands appear as Modal documentation examples, including signup links, dashboard links, curl examples, and Modal CLI commands. No evidence found that the skill itself makes hidden network requests or executes these commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The patterns are inside markdown examples and product documentation. They are user-visible instructions, not hidden executable behavior in the skill package.

dangerous_patterns.title: Image Build Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates Modal image build commands that can run shell commands while building containers. Users should pin dependencies and review every command before building images.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit in the documentation and is a normal Modal feature. It is dangerous only when commands or repositories are untrusted.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Launch Inside Cloud Function
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates subprocess usage inside a GPU function and a web server function. The examples use list arguments and include a warning against shell=True, reducing command injection risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct subprocess patterns are present, but the arguments are static and a security warning is included. This supports medium concern, not a confirmed vulnerability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Access Through Environment Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates accessing API keys and authentication tokens from environment variables. This is required for Modal secrets, but outputs and logs must not expose these values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The referenced examples clearly access secrets from os.environ. The surrounding documentation frames this as secret management rather than credential theft.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Directory Inclusion in Container Images
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates adding local directories into Modal images. This can leak local files into remote containers if users choose credential directories or broad home paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real and security-sensitive because local paths are copied or uploaded. The examples are instructional and do not prove malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Teams need a clear way to move Python, ML, and batch workloads from local scripts to cloud compute. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Modal setup, deployment, scaling, storage, secrets, GPUs, and endpoints.
actual_capabilities: Explains Modal authentication, app creation, local runs, and persistent deployments.
Shows how to define Modal images with Python packages, system packages, registries, and build steps.
Guides GPU selection for inference, training, multi-GPU jobs, and fallback configurations.
Documents Modal functions, classes, remote calls, parallel maps, spawning, and autoscaling settings.
Covers volumes, secrets, web endpoints, FastAPI integration, and scheduled jobs.
Provides scientific computing examples for ML inference, batch processing, simulations, and data collection.
limitations: It does not provision a Modal account or validate billing limits for the user.
It cannot guarantee that copied examples are secure for production without review.
Some reference content uses placeholders and example endpoints that require adaptation.
It does not replace Modal documentation for current quotas, pricing, or product changes.

use_cases.title: Serve a GPU Model
use_cases.description: Package model dependencies, choose a GPU, load weights, and expose prediction methods or endpoints on Modal.

use_cases.title: Run Parallel Batch Processing
use_cases.description: Convert local data processing scripts into Modal functions that map work across many containers.

use_cases.title: Operate Scheduled Cloud Jobs
use_cases.description: Create cron or periodic Modal functions that fetch data, write volumes, and report failures.

prompt_templates.title: Create a First Modal App
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a minimal Modal app for a Python function. Include setup steps, the file structure, and the command to run it locally.

prompt_templates.title: Package Dependencies for Modal
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Modal image for my Python workload with these dependencies: [list packages]. Pin risky dependencies and explain where local imports should happen.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy a GPU Inference Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert my model inference script into a Modal service with GPU selection, model loading, secrets for tokens, and autoscaling settings.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Production Modal Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Modal design for production readiness. Check secrets, public endpoints, image build commands, volumes, schedules, scaling limits, and cost controls.

output_examples.input: User asks for a Modal plan to deploy a transformer model on one GPU.
output: A deployment plan with image dependencies, GPU choice, model loading lifecycle, and endpoint shape.
Security notes for Hugging Face tokens, public access, request limits, and logging.
Operational notes for cold starts, autoscaling, timeout, and cost monitoring.

output_examples.input: User asks how to process thousands of files in parallel.
output: A Modal function design that maps file identifiers across containers.
Guidance for using volumes or object storage for inputs and outputs.
Recommendations for retries, concurrency limits, and idempotent processing.

output_examples.input: User asks for a scheduled data collection job.
output: A cron-based Modal job design with secrets, volume writes, and failure handling.
A checklist for API key storage, response validation, and avoiding duplicate writes.

best_practices: Review every Modal command before running it because examples can deploy cloud resources.
Use Modal Secrets for credentials and avoid printing or hardcoding secret values.
Set explicit limits for GPUs, containers, timeouts, retries, and endpoint authentication.
anti_patterns: Do not copy local credential directories into images or volumes without a strict allowlist.
Do not expose Modal web endpoints publicly before adding authentication and input validation.
Do not leave expensive GPU jobs or high container limits running without budget monitoring.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps build Modal apps for serverless Python, GPU inference, batch jobs, web endpoints, schedules, volumes, and secrets.

faq.question: Does it run Modal commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. The user or agent must explicitly run any Modal command.

faq.question: Can it help choose a GPU?
faq.answer: Yes. It summarizes Modal GPU options and patterns for inference, training, multi-GPU use, and fallback choices.

faq.question: How should I handle secrets?
faq.answer: Use Modal Secrets, keep credentials out of source files, avoid broad environment exposure, and never print secret values.

faq.question: Is this safe for production endpoints?
faq.answer: It can guide production endpoints, but you must add authentication, validation, rate controls, monitoring, and deployment review.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it teaches cloud execution, public endpoints, secrets, file uploads, and persistent jobs.
